architecture.summary: CFPRM is a data collection and training strategy for Process Reward Models (PRMs) in mathematical reasoning. It operates on step sequences generated by LLMs: a sliding window of size w initially merges consecutive reasoning steps into coarse-grained steps (labeled by the last step's label), then w is progressively decreased until reaching 1, collecting training data at multiple granularities. All collected data (coarse and fine-grained) are combined and used to train a PRM end-to-end via standard supervised learning objectives (BCE, MSE, or Q-ranking) applied to the LLM backbone. At inference, the trained PRM scores individual steps.
trained_components: Process Reward Model (PRM) fine-tuned on multi-granularity step sequences

biblio.title: Coarse-to-Fine Process Reward Modeling for Mathematical Reasoning

claims.statement: Reasoning steps generated by LLMs often fail to exhibit strictly incremental information, leading to redundancy that can hinder effective reasoning.
CFPRM improves upon ReSTMCTS* by 1.3% on GSM-Plus and 3.4% on MATH500 when employing MSE as the loss function
CFPRM consistently brings performance improvements across all configurations (BoN@8, BoN@16, BoN@32, BoN@64) across three loss criteria (BCE, MSE, Q-ranking) on two datasets
When integrating CFPRM with the Q-ranking-based method (PQM), performance on GSM-Plus averages to 71.3% (BoN) and on MATH500 averages to 84.1% (BoN)
The Q-ranking-based method is more sensitive to window size w; when w is set to 4, performance on GSM-Plus shows no improvement over baseline, and performance on MATH500 lags behind the baseline
Setting w to 2 or 3 can better boost overall Q-ranking performance; the optimal merge window size is not fixed across different loss criteria
The optimal window size w should be designed adaptively with respect to different loss criteria, which is not addressed in this work
A more reasonable method should involve detecting and removing redundant steps, which is not discussed in this work
